_readme: Golden set transcribed from Adrian's own red-pen marking (scans in ~/Desktop/AdrianMath/marking_calibration/). expected.line_verdicts aligns 1:1 with working_lines (null = don't care). expected.must_mention = case-insensitive substrings the marking JSON must contain somewhere (correction/summary). Grow this set as more scripts are transcribed — especially items where the current prompt disagrees with Adrian.

items.source: EM-circle-properties-circular-measure p1 Q1a
items.question: G is a point outside circle with centre O. GA and GB are tangents to the circle at A and B. Explain why a circle can be drawn passing through A, O, B and G, stating the diameter.
working_lines: The centre will be midpoint of GO
items.note: Adrian accepted the midpoint claim but supplied the missing justification chain in red: ∠GAO = ∠GBO = 90° (tan ⊥ rad), right angles in semicircle, AB... circle through A,O,B,G with GO as diameter. A bare assertion without the tangent-radius reason is an incomplete explanation.

items.source: EM-circle-properties-circular-measure p1 Q1b(ii)
items.question: In the circle with centre O, ∠AOB = 126°. P, C are points on the circumference. Find ∠OAB and hence ∠DCA, giving reasons.
items.note: Fully correct with reasons stated — Adrian ticks every line, no deduction, no commentary.

items.source: AM-plane-geometry p1 Q2b
items.question: In the diagram, K is the midpoint of QR, H is the midpoint of PH and HR... Given PR = 2PH and QH = HS, prove that PQ = SR.
items.note: Adrian: 'does not prove PQ = SR' — restating givens then asserting the conclusion is a logical gap worth all the marks; his red pen supplies the midpoint-theorem chain (PQ = 2HK and SR = 2HK ∴ PQ = SR).

items.source: EM-Prelim-Paper-Set-1-Paper-1 Q7
items.question: ABCD is a parallelogram. APQ, BRQ, CRS and DPS are straight lines which bisect angles A, B, C and D respectively. Show that triangle ADP and triangle CBR are congruent. Give a reason for each statement you make.
items.note: Adrian marked −2 of [3]: first statement fine (he tightened the reason to 'opp sides of parallelogram'), second is wrong (not vertically opposite), and the attempt never reaches a congruency test. His red pen writes the complete model proof ending '∴ △ADP ≡ △CBR (ASA)'.

items.source: EM-coordinate-geometry-graphs p3 Q3ii
items.question: Line AB: y = 2x + h. Line BC: x + 2y = 12h. B is the intersection of AB and BC. Find the coordinates of B in terms of h.
items.note: The student mis-copied 12h where rearranging gives 6h. Adrian circles the wrong token, writes 6 above, then ECF: the subsequent solving METHOD is followed through as correct (his red continuation shows x=2h, y=5h, B(2h,5h)). One error, one deduction — later lines are not re-penalized.

items.source: EM-circle-properties-circular-measure IMG_9169 Q1a; printed question: 'O REV 2026 EM 4' PDF p4, Circular Measure Practice Q1(a). Re-transcribed 2026-07-15 with the real question text + full working (the earlier paraphrase omitted the tangent-tangent configuration and truncated the working to 2 lines — no model would full-forfeit without seeing that D is external).
items.question: ACB is an arc of a circle with centre O and radius 8 cm. AD and BD are tangents to the circle at A and B respectively, meeting at the external point D, and angle ADB = π/4. (a) Find the length of AD. [2]
working_lines: reflex ∠AOB = (π/4) × 2 = π/2 (∠ at centre is 2 times ∠ at circumference)
items.note: Adrian: underlines the cited property, ✗, 'this property is not relevant in this question' (D is an external point — no circumference angle exists, the property cannot apply), then '✗✗ discuss in class and try again'. Full forfeit −2 of [2]: every subsequent line manipulates the value produced by the misapplied property, so none of it earns method marks. Correct path: quadrilateral DAOB has right angles at A and B (tan ⊥ rad) so ∠AOB = 3π/4; right triangle ADO with ∠ADO = π/8 gives AD = 8/tan(π/8) ≈ 19.3 cm.

items.source: Kylie_2020_EOY.pdf p2 Q5a (TJC Aurora 2024, Sec 2). max_marks assumed [2] — printed paper not in folder.
items.question: Factorise completely: −6b²c − 18b²c² − 12b³c
items.note: Student dropped the 1 term inside the bracket (−6b²c × 1 = −6b²c is unaccounted for). Adrian inserts a red '1 +' with a caret into the bracket and writes a generalizing teaching example in the margin: 'eg. 2a + 4a²b = 2a(1 + 2ab)'. One slip, −1; minimal-token fix, not a re-derivation.

items.source: Kylie_2020_EOY.pdf p2 Q5b (TJC Aurora 2024, Sec 2). max_marks assumed [2].
items.question: Express as a single fraction in its simplest form: (3−2x)/15 − [−(2x+5)/5 + (x−4)/10]
items.note: Three correct rearrangement lines, then the x-terms are mis-combined on the final line: −4x+12x−3x = 5x, student wrote 13x. Adrian circles the 13, ✗, writes the corrected fraction (5x+48)/30 beside it. Single arithmetic slip on the last line → −1; earlier lines all ticked.

items.source: Kylie_2020_EOY.pdf p4 Q9b(iii) (TJC Aurora 2024, Sec 2). QUESTION RECONSTRUCTED from parts (a)/(b) of the student's working — Adrian to verify against the Aurora paper. max_marks assumed [2].
items.question: In the pattern, the sum of row n is n² + 2n + 1 = (n+1)² (established in part (b)). The sums of two consecutive rows differ by 97. Find the sum of each of these two rows.
items.note: The 2n+3 difference is correct in value but asserted with no derivation (and with confusing notation — Tn was defined as 2n+1 in part (a), for which Tn+1−Tn = 2, not 2n+3): Adrian writes 'how did you get 2n+3?' pointing at line 1. Solving to n=47 is ticked through. The final answer gives the row INDICES (47, 48) instead of the requested sums — red pen supplies n+2 = 49, (n+1)² = 48² = 2304, (n+2)² = 49² = 2401. Net margin −1 of [2]: the solve earns its mark; the unjustified setup + wrong final interpretation costs the other.

items.source: Kylie_2020_EOY.pdf p4 Q10(i) (TJC Aurora 2024, Sec 2). QUESTION RECONSTRUCTED — diagram not available; Adrian to verify. max_marks assumed [1].
items.question: In the diagram, straight lines HI and AB intersect at G, and ∠BGI = 23°. Find ∠HGA, giving a reason.
items.note: Correct value with an imprecise reason: 'opposite angles' should be 'vertically opposite angles'. Adrian inserts a red 'vertically' with a caret and double-ticks the line — the reason wording is tightened IN PASSING with NO deduction and verdict stays correct. Full marks, empty margin. This is the equivalent-wording control: a marker that flips this line to 'wrong' or deducts is over-marking.

items.source: Tiffany_S2Math_PracticePaper.pdf p1 Q1 (digital iPad marking — green ink is Adrian). max_marks assumed [2].
items.question: By rounding each number to 1 significant figure, estimate the value of (3456 × 0.987) / 25.
items.note: Full marks — every line ticked. Leniency control: this is an ESTIMATION question; 3456→3000, 0.987→1, 25→30 (1 s.f.) is exactly what was asked. A marker that penalizes 25→30 for 'inaccuracy' or demands the exact value 136.5 is mis-marking the question type.

items.source: KayLen_ Numbers Advanced Practice.pdf p3 Q4; printed question + [2] + answer on p1.
items.question: By approximating each number to 2 significant figures, estimate the value of (12.1 × √48.8) / ∛27.3. [2]
items.note: Full marks, every line ticked including the answer. Clean estimation execution: approximate under the radicals to perfect powers (48.8→49, 27.3→27), then evaluate. Empty margin.

items.source: KayLen_ Numbers Advanced Practice.pdf p3 Q5; printed question + [1] + answer on p1.
items.question: The number 102 999 corrected to n significant figures is 103 000. Write down the largest possible value of n. [1]
items.note: Answer-only response on a [1] 'write down' question — correct value, full marks, ticked. Control: 'write down' questions need no working; demanding shown working here is over-marking.

items.source: KayLen_ Numbers Advanced Practice.pdf p2 Q2; printed question + [3] + answer (x=8.4, y=21) on p1.
items.question: A rectangular board can be exactly covered by 12 identical rectangular cards arranged as shown (cards of width x and length y; the arrangement makes the board length 6x and gives y = 2.5x). The length of the board is 50.4 cm. Find the length and width of each card. [3]
items.note: Adrian circles the question number and writes 'needs clearer explanation' — but deducts NOTHING (both answers right, printed answer matches). Presentation feedback rides alongside full marks. A marker that converts 'unclear setup' into a deduction here is over-marking; the right behaviour is full marks + the comment (uncertainty note or summary remark is acceptable).

items.source: KayLen_ Numbers Advanced Practice.pdf p2 Q3(i); printed question + [1] + answer (148.6 m) on p1.
items.question: A rectangular field is measured as 49.9 m and 24.5 m, correct to the nearest 0.1 metre. Find (i) the least possible perimeter in metres. [1]
items.note: The student's '≈' notation on the first two lines is written backwards (49.85 IS the least value of a quantity recorded as 49.9 — it is not 'approximately 49.9'); Adrian crosses that notation. But the bounds themselves are the right values and the perimeter line is correct and ticked: 148.6, full [1], empty margin. Notation repaired in passing, not penalized — the deduction-free ✗ on a notation line must not pull the margin down when the required computation is right.

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p1 Q3(ii) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: Given that one solution of the equation 1/(2x² + kx) = 1/2 is x = −2: in part (i) the student correctly found k = 3. Part (ii): find a second possible value of x. [1]
items.note: Adrian: 'why sub k=0?' at line 1 and 'Sub k=3!' — the value of k was already determined in (i); substituting k=0 answers a different equation. Red rework: 1/(2x²+3x) = 1/2 → 2x²+3x−2 = 0 → (2x−1)(x+2) = 0 → x = 1/2 (or the given −2). −1 of [1].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p2 Q5 (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: Given that 2m − 1 = (2n + 3)², where n is a positive integer, show that m is an integer. [2]
items.note: Verifying one value of n does not show the statement — Adrian: 'need to prove for all values of n.' (−2, full forfeit). His red model proof: (2n+3)² = (even + odd)² = (odd)², an odd number, so 2m−1 is odd → 2m = odd + 1 = even → m is an integer.

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p6 Q6 (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: The graph shows the trend in the number of coronavirus infections in a country, plotted with a vertical axis marked 10, 100, 1000, 10000 (equal spacing between successive powers of 10) against dates. The plotted trend looks roughly like a straight line. Jamie claims the trend is approximated by a linear equation of the form y = 320x, where x is the number of days and y is the number of infections. Explain why she is wrong. [1]
working_lines: The number of infections per day is inconsistent.
items.note: Adrian: 'how so? be clearer.' — the sentence does not identify the actual reason. Red model answer: 'The scale on the vertical axis is not uniform, hence the graph is not linear.' On an explain question the explanation IS the answer; a vague response that never names the mathematical reason earns nothing: −1 of [1].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p6 Q7 (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: There are 30 members in a community club. All members take up at least one activity, either aqua aerobics or pickleball. 15 members take up aqua aerobics and 24 take up pickleball. A = {members who take up aqua aerobics}, P = {members who take up pickleball}. Indicate in the Venn diagram, showing clearly, the number of members in each subset. [2] (The student filled the three regions of the Venn diagram as transcribed below.)
items.note: Overlap 15 + 24 − 30 = 9 and P-only 24 − 9 = 15 are right; the A-only region must be 15 − 9 = 6, but the student re-used the whole-set count 15. Adrian crosses the 15, writes 6 below it, ticks the other two regions: −1 of [2].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p4 Q11 (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: The picture shows a model terracotta warrior. The model has a height of 15 cm and weighs 20 grammes. A similar terracotta warrior has a height of 1.8 metres. Find the weight of the larger terracotta warrior. Give your answer in kilogrammes, correct to 1 decimal place. [3]
items.note: Weight was scaled by the LINEAR ratio. Adrian: 'weight is related to volume!' and 'For similar solids, weight is not equal to volume, but their ratios are.' — the weight ratio equals the CUBE of the length ratio. Full forfeit −3 of [3]; red rework: W/0.02 = (180/15)³ → W = (180/15)³ × 0.02 = 34.6 kg (1 d.p.).

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p5 Q12(b) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: Given that R varies inversely as the square of T, find the percentage change in R when T is doubled. [3]
items.note: The manipulation to new R = R/4 is correct, but the question asks for the PERCENTAGE CHANGE and the student stopped without computing it — no percentage, no sign, answer line untouched. Adrian circled −3 (full forfeit of [3]) and completed the work in red: (R − R/4)/R × 100 = 75, so R decreases by 75% (percentage change −75%). The asked-for quantity never appearing forfeits the part despite correct preliminary algebra.

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p8 Q15(ii) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: x₁, x₂, x₃, …, x₁₀ are 10 unique numbers whose mean x̄ is 11.8 and standard deviation is 4.729. Find the value of x₁² + x₂² + x₃² + … + x₁₀², giving your answer to the nearest whole number. [2]
items.note: The student treated Σx² as n × (standard deviation)², dropping the mean term from σ² = Σx²/n − x̄². Adrian's red rework: 4.729² = Σx²/10 − 11.8² → Σx²/10 = 4.729² + 139.24 = 161.603441 → Σx² = 1616 (nearest whole number). Wrong formula → full forfeit −2 of [2].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p10 Q17(ii) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: Given that y = x² + 6x + c, make x the subject of the formula. [3] (Part (i) asked to express x² + 6x + 10 in the form (x + p)² + q.)
items.note: Line 3 square-roots the left side TERM BY TERM (√(x² + 6x) is not x + 6x) — an illegal operation, not a slip; everything after rests on it. Full forfeit −3 of [3]. Adrian's red model: complete the square — y = (x+3)² − 9 + c → (x+3)² = y + 9 − c → x + 3 = ±√(y + 9 − c) → x = −3 ± √(y + 9 − c).

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p11 Q19(ii) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: A(1, 2), B(8, 5), C(13, 7) and D(13, 2) are four points. In part (i) the student correctly showed AB + BC = 13.0, correct to 3 significant figures. Part (ii): By finding AC, determine the sum of interior angles in figure ABCD, justifying your answer. [3]
working_lines: ∴ ABCD is a right ∠ △, total sum of interior ∠s = 180°
items.note: AC = 13 is right, but calling four-lettered ABCD a triangle requires showing B lies ON segment AC — the load-bearing justification is AB + BC = 13.0 = AC, so A, B and C lie on a straight line and ABCD degenerates to triangle ACD (interior angle sum 180°). Adrian: 'We need to first find out if A, B and C is on a straight line… It looks like the points are on a straight line, but since the question didn't say, we should not assume.' Right final number with the justification absent on a justify question → full forfeit −3 of [3].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p12 Q21(i) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: A(−16, 0), B(0, 12) and C(8, 2) are three points. Find the equation of the line AB, expressing your answer in the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b and c are constants to be found. [2]
items.note: Gradient 3/4 and y = (3/4)x + 12 are both right (B(0,12) is the y-intercept). The final rearrangement is botched — y turned into 12y and the signs never moved: from y = (3/4)x + 12, 4y = 3x + 48 → 3x − 4y + 48 = 0. One slip in the last line → −1 of [2].

items.source: Pearle_EM_Set3_P1.pdf p13 Q22(i) (EM 2022 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 1)
items.question: Checkpoints W, X, Y are in a jungle reserve. X is due north of Y and is on a bearing of 077° from W; the angle between the north line at X and the line XW is 103°. Angle XWY = 53° and WY is 60 metres. Calculate the distance XY. [2]
items.note: Sine-rule setup fully correct and ticked; the numeric evaluation is a calculator slip — (60/sin 77°) × sin 53° = 49.17857 → 49.2 m (3 s.f.), the student got 23.766. Adrian crosses the value, writes 'calculation error.' and the corrected 49.2: −1 of [2] with the method marks kept.

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p2 Q3(a) (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: The stem-and-leaf diagram shows the masses, in grams, of some apples. Stems (tens): 19 | 1 4 5 5; 20 | 0 3 4 7 8 8; 21 | 0 1 4 7 7 8 9; 22 | 4 6 8. Key: 19 | 4 represents 194 grams. For these masses, find (a) the range. [1]
items.note: The student subtracted the quartiles (Q3 − Q1 = 217 − 203) — that is the interquartile range, not the range. Adrian underlines 'the range' in the question, writes 'not IQR.', crosses the 14 and gives the correct range 228 − 191 = 37: −1 of [1].

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p2 Q3(b) (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: The stem-and-leaf diagram shows the masses, in grams, of some apples. Stems (tens): 19 | 1 4 5 5; 20 | 0 3 4 7 8 8; 21 | 0 1 4 7 7 8 9; 22 | 4 6 8. Key: 19 | 4 represents 194 grams. For these masses, find (b) the median. [1]
items.note: There are 20 values (even count), so the median is the average of the 10th and 11th values: (208 + 210)/2 = 209. The student answered 208 (the 10th value). Adrian crosses 208 and writes 209: −1 of [1].

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p4 Q6(a) (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: ξ = {integers x : 1 ≤ x ≤ 12}. From the Venn diagram: A = {1, 2, 5, 10}, B = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11}, C = {2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11}. Use one of the symbols ∅, ⊂, ⊄, ∉, ∈, ξ to complete the statement: {2, 10} ___ A. [1]
items.note: {2, 10} is a SET of two elements of A, so the correct symbol is ⊂ (a proper subset of A); ∈ relates a single element to a set. Adrian crosses the answer and writes ⊂: −1 of [1]. The set-vs-element symbol confusion is the exact concept being tested.

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p4 Q7 (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: Parveen runs a squash club. 58 of the members are adults and 37 are children. Her aim is that at least 70% of the members should be adults. Work out the smallest number of adults that would need to join the club in order to achieve her aim. [3]
items.note: The student computed 70% of the CURRENT total 95, ignoring that each joining adult also raises the total membership. Adrian circles the 95, writes 'you did not consider the people joining.', crosses 67 and gives 29: full forfeit −3 of [3]. Correct chain: (58 + x)/(95 + x) ≥ 0.7 → 0.3x ≥ 8.5 → x ≥ 28.3, so 29 adults.

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p8 Q15 (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: In the diagram, A, B, C and D are points on a circle, centre O. AC passes through O. AB = 8.1 cm, BC = 13.8 cm and angle BDA = x°. Find x. Show your working and give reasons. [4]
items.note: AB = 8.1 and BC = 13.8 are the two LEGS of the right triangle (right angle at B), so the ratio is tan ∠ACB = 8.1/13.8, not sin. Adrian circles 'sin', writes 'tan', and corrects the answer to x = 30.4 (tan⁻¹(8.1/13.8) = 30.4°). The semicircle reason and the same-segment transfer both earned their marks: −2 of [4], not a full forfeit.

items.source: LiuYing_EM_TYS2017_P1.pdf p15 Q23(a) (GCE O-Level 4048/01 Oct/Nov 2017 TYS)
items.question: A company sells three sizes of the same brand of shampoo: 400 ml at $30.80, 250 ml at $19.25, and 75 ml at $5.95. Show that the cost of the shampoo is not directly proportional to the quantity of shampoo. [2]
working_lines: Assume that cost is directly proportional to the quantity of shampoo
∴ cost of shampoo is not directly proportional to the quantity of shampoo
items.note: The working uses A = ka and A = k/a (inverse proportion) interchangeably — Adrian circles both forms, writes 'which is it?' and 'There are 2 sets of workings that tell separate stories.', and forfeits both marks even though the final sentence states the right conclusion. A 'show that' must present ONE coherent argument (e.g. compare unit rates 30.80/400 = 0.077 vs 5.95/75 = 0.0793 — different, so not directly proportional); self-contradictory working earns nothing.

items.source: JunHe_AM_SGSS_P1.pdf p5 Q6(iii) (SGSS AMP1/4E Prelim 2021, AM)
items.question: A certain species of fish was introduced into a pond and its population P, t days later, is given by P = 5000 + Ae^(kt). From parts (i)–(ii): A = −2000 and k = −0.0511 (3 s.f.), so P = 5000 − 2000e^(−0.0511t). (iii) Explain why the number of fishes can never reach 5000. [1]
working_lines: For all values of t, −2000e^(−0.0511t) can never be 0.
items.note: Both sentences are true, but the explanation never makes the link explicit — Adrian: 'does not adequately explain.' (−1 of [1]) and writes the required chain in red: let P = 5000 → 5000 = 5000 − 2000e^(−0.0511t) → 0 = −2000e^(−0.0511t) → e^(−0.0511t) = 0, impossible since an exponential is always positive — 'need this part for a more complete explanation.' On explain questions the explicit derivation from the target value IS the answer.

items.source: JunHe_AM_SGSS_P1.pdf p9 Q10(ii) (SGSS AMP1/4E Prelim 2021, AM)
items.question: Given that y = x(2x − 4)³, the stationary points found in part (i) are (2, 0) and (1/2, −27/2). (ii) Determine the nature of each of these points. [4]
items.note: d²y/dx² = 0 at a stationary point is INCONCLUSIVE — Adrian crosses the inflexion conclusion and writes '★ d²y/dx² = 0 → does not mean it is a point of inflexion! Need to use sign-test to determine', supplying a first-derivative sign-test table (x = 2⁻, 2, 2⁺; dy/dx +ve, 0, +ve → stationary point of inflexion). The conclusion happens to be right but is unestablished: −1 of [4]. The minimum at x = 1/2 (72 > 0) is fully correct and keeps its marks.

items.source: JunHe_AM_SGSS_P1.pdf p10 Q11(i) (SGSS AMP1/4E Prelim 2021, AM)
items.question: A particle P moves along a horizontal straight line so that t seconds after motion has begun, its displacement s m from a fixed point O is given by s = t³ + 5t² − 2t + 4. (i) Express the velocity of P in terms of t. [1]
items.note: d/dt(5t²) = +10t; the student flipped the sign to −10t. Adrian crosses the line and writes v = 3t² + 10t − 2 in red: −1 of [1] (a slip, but on a 1-mark part it costs the mark).

items.source: JunHe_AM_SGSS_P1.pdf p12 Q12(a)(i) (SGSS AMP1/4E Prelim 2021, AM)
items.question: (a)(i) Show that x² − 10x + 21 can never be smaller than −4. [2]
working_lines: since discriminant > 0, x² − 10x + 21 can never be smaller than −4 [shown]
items.note: The discriminant of x² − 10x + 21 itself (16 > 0 — the curve crosses the x-axis twice) says NOTHING about how low the curve goes; the inference is a non-sequitur and Adrian crosses it with a large ✗: full forfeit −2 of [2]. His red supplies the valid route: complete the square — x² − 10x + 21 = (x − 5)² − 4, and since (x − 5)² ≥ 0, the expression is ≥ −4 for all x (minimum −4 at x = 5).

items.source: JunHe_AM_Set6_P2.pdf p3 Q2(ii) (AM 2021 Prelim Set 6 Paper 2)
items.question: In part (i) the identity (tan²y − sin²y)/(sec²y(cosec²y − 1)) = tan²y sin⁴y was proven. (ii) Hence solve the equation (tan²y − sin²y)/(sec²y(cosec²y − 1)) = (1/16)tan²y for 0° ≤ y ≤ 180°. [4]
working_lines: when sin y < 0: y = 210°, 330° (out of range)
items.note: Dividing both sides by tan²y throws away the tan²y = 0 roots. Adrian: 'don't divide by tan²y. factorise' with the red chain tan²y(sin⁴y − 1/16) = 0 → tan y = 0 giving y = 0°, 180°, and '★ you are missing these answers': −2 of [4]. The 30°/150° branch is ticked — the deduction is for the lost solution family, not the found one.

items.source: Fenglin_EM_PracticeSet3_Paper2.pdf p1 Q1(c) (EM 2021 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 2)
items.question: (c) Solve the equation 1/((x+1)(x−2)) = 2/(8−x). [4]
items.note: The student jumps from 2x² − x − 12 = 0 straight to decimal-root 'factors' (calculator solve written as a factorisation). Adrian TICKS the final roots (2.71 and −2.21 are correct to 3 s.f.), deducts NOTHING, and writes 'does your school allow this?' beside the step plus the standard quadratic-formula working in red. Method-policy feedback rides along as a query/uncertainty note — a marker that deducts here or flips the roots to wrong is over-marking.

items.source: Fenglin_EM_PracticeSet3_Paper2.pdf p3 Q2(b)(i) (EM 2021 Prelims Practice Set 3, Paper 2)
items.question: Paul bought an apartment at the end of 2006. The price of the apartment at the end of 2006 was 9% higher than at the end of 2005. The price at the end of 2007 was 6% higher than at the end of 2006. (i) Express the price of the apartment at the end of 2007 as a percentage of the price at the end of 2005. [1]
items.note: Successive percentage increases MULTIPLY, they don't add — Adrian: 'percentage does not work like that. Do year by year.' Red model: let the 2005 price be x → 2006 is 1.09x → 2007 is 1.09x × 1.06 = 1.1554x → 115.54%. Crossed 115, wrote 115.54: −1 of [1].
